Saturn V's five first stage engines burned about 15 tons of fuel and oxidizer every single second at liftoff.
For over half a century the Saturn V remained the most powerful rocket ever successfully flown, until SpaceX's Starship finally beat it in 2023.
Apollo astronauts flew to the Moon on a guidance computer with about 4 kilobytes of working memory.
Apollo's computer memory was woven by hand, with workers threading copper wires through tiny magnetic rings to store each bit of the software.
Launch pads flood themselves with hundreds of thousands of gallons of water at liftoff, mostly to soak up sound energy that could shake the rocket apart.
Mars Climate Orbiter burned up in 1999 because one engineering team worked in pounds of force while another worked in newtons.
Ariane 5 exploded on its first flight in 1996 because it reused guidance software from the smaller Ariane 4, and a number overflowed 16 bits.
Hubble's main mirror was ground with exquisite precision to slightly the wrong shape, an error around one fiftieth the width of a human hair.
NASA never spent millions on a space pen. Fisher developed it privately and sold pens to NASA for a few dollars each.
SR-71 Blackbirds leaked fuel on the runway by design. The panels only sealed once the airframe heated up and expanded at Mach 3.
America secretly sourced much of the titanium for its SR-71 spy plane from the Soviet Union, the very country the plane was built to spy on.
Concorde stretched up to 25 centimeters in flight as air friction heated its aluminum skin.
Flying west, Concorde outpaced the sun across the sky, so passengers could watch a sunset run backwards.
Voyager 1 is the farthest human made object, more than 15 billion miles away and still phoning home on 1970s hardware.
By the time Voyager's radio signal reaches Earth, it arrives billions of times weaker than the power of a watch battery.
Parker Solar Probe has reached roughly 430,000 miles per hour, making it the fastest machine humans have ever built.
An ion engine's thrust is about the weight of a sheet of paper resting on your palm, yet it can push a spacecraft for years without stopping.
GPS satellites must correct for Einstein's relativity. Without the fix, position errors would grow by about 10 kilometers every day.
Without embedded cooling pipes, the concrete in Hoover Dam would have taken more than a century to cool and cure.
Hoover Dam contains enough concrete to pave a two lane highway from San Francisco to New York.
Filling the reservoir behind China's Three Gorges Dam moved so much water that it lengthened Earth's day by a tiny fraction of a microsecond.
Panama Canal locks are filled entirely by gravity. Water simply flows downhill from an artificial lake, with no pumps at all.
Golden Gate Bridge's two main cables contain about 80,000 miles of wire, enough to circle the Earth three times.
In 1884, P T Barnum marched 21 elephants across the Brooklyn Bridge to prove to a nervous public that it was safe.
Building the Brooklyn Bridge introduced doctors to the bends. Workers in pressurized caissons surfaced too fast and fell mysteriously ill.
Tacoma Narrows Bridge twisted itself to pieces in a 40 mile per hour wind in 1940. The only fatality was a dog named Tubby.
France's Millau Viaduct stands taller than the Eiffel Tower, with its tallest mast reaching 343 meters above the valley floor.
An earthquake struck while Japan's Akashi Kaikyo Bridge was under construction and pushed its towers so far apart the span had to be lengthened by a meter.
China's Danyang to Kunshan Grand Bridge stretches about 165 kilometers, so long that a high speed train needs over half an hour to cross it.
In 1952, London's Tower Bridge began opening with a double decker bus still on it. The driver accelerated and jumped the gap.
On hot days the steel arch of the Sydney Harbour Bridge expands, lifting the roadway by as much as 18 centimeters.
Burj Khalifa's builders pumped liquid concrete more than 600 meters straight up, a world record at the time.
Air conditioning in the Burj Khalifa condenses so much water out of the desert air that the runoff irrigates the gardens around the tower.
The Eiffel Tower grows about 15 centimeters taller in summer as its iron expands in the heat.
Paris nearly demolished the Eiffel Tower in 1909. It survived largely because it made a superb radio antenna.
Melt down the entire Eiffel Tower and pour the iron across its own base, and the puddle would be only about 6 centimeters deep.
Empire State Building crews finished the tower in just 410 days, with the steel frame rising four and a half floors in a single week at its peak.
A student's question in 1978 revealed that New York's Citicorp tower could topple in a strong storm, triggering months of secret night welding.
Kansas City's Hyatt Regency walkways collapsed in 1981 after a small change to a hanger rod detail quietly doubled the load on one connection.
London's Millennium Bridge closed two days after opening because pedestrians unconsciously synced their steps with its sway and made it worse.
Sweden's warship Vasa, pride of the navy, sailed about 1,300 meters on her 1628 maiden voyage before capsizing in front of the crowd.
A storage tank burst in Boston in 1919 and sent a wave of molasses through the streets fast enough to knock buildings off their foundations.
Kansai International Airport sits on an artificial island that has sunk more than 11 meters since construction began.
Engineers quietly pulled the Leaning Tower of Pisa back by about 40 centimeters in the 1990s, enough to keep it standing for centuries more.
Channel Tunnel crews digging from England and France met under the sea in 1990, misaligned by only about the length of a shoe.
Switzerland's Gotthard Base Tunnel runs 57 kilometers through the Alps with up to 2.3 kilometers of solid rock overhead.
Magnets in the Large Hadron Collider are chilled to 1.9 kelvin, colder than the empty space between galaxies.
Protons in the Large Hadron Collider move at 99.9999991 percent of light speed, lapping the 27 kilometer ring more than 11,000 times a second.
One giant marine diesel, the Wartsila RT-flex96C, stands as tall as a four story building and produces about 109,000 horsepower.
Huge low speed ship engines convert about half their fuel's energy into motion, roughly twice the efficiency of a typical car engine.
Bagger 288, a German bucket wheel excavator weighing around 13,000 tons, once drove itself 22 kilometers overland to a new mine.
NASA's crawler transporter hauls rockets to the pad at one mile per hour and manages about 32 feet per gallon of diesel.
Bertha, then the world's largest tunneling machine, broke down under Seattle in 2013 and sat stuck underground for about two years.
Turbine blades inside a jet engine run in gas hotter than their own melting point, surviving on a thin cushion of cooling air.
Each high pressure turbine blade in a large jet engine extracts roughly the power of a Formula 1 car from the passing gas.
Many jet engine turbine blades are grown as a single metal crystal, because ordinary grain boundaries would tear apart under the stress.
At takeoff, one engine on a big airliner swallows enough air to empty a squash court every second.
A diesel locomotive is really an electric one. The diesel engine only spins a generator, and electric motors turn the wheels.
Over 2,000 years ago, Greek craftsmen built the Antikythera mechanism, a geared bronze machine that predicted eclipses.
James Watt invented the unit of horsepower as marketing, so mine owners could compare his steam engines to the horses they replaced.
Aerogel is more than 99 percent air, and NASA used it to catch comet dust particles moving faster than rifle bullets.
Old window panes are thicker at the bottom because of how they were made, not because glass slowly flows. It doesn't.
A Prince Rupert's drop of glass can shrug off a hammer blow to its head, yet explodes into powder if you snap its thin tail.
Stainless steel was discovered in 1913 by Harry Brearley, who noticed his discarded gun barrel test pieces refused to rust.
Roman concrete can heal its own cracks. Embedded lime fragments react with water and reseal the damage over time.
Nearly 2,000 years after it was poured, the Pantheon in Rome still holds the record for the largest unreinforced concrete dome.
Concrete is the most used human made material on Earth. Only water is consumed in greater quantities.
Cement making alone produces around 8 percent of the world's carbon dioxide emissions.
Graphene is a sheet of carbon a single atom thick, yet gram for gram it is about 200 times stronger than steel.
Metal whiskers can sprout spontaneously from tin plated electronics and short out circuits. They have killed entire satellites.
In deep cold, pure tin can slowly crumble into gray powder, a failure mode engineers call tin pest.
Extreme ultraviolet chip machines blast 50,000 droplets of molten tin per second with lasers just to generate their light.
A single top end chipmaking machine costs over 150 million dollars and ships to customers in multiple jumbo jet loads.
Transistors on a modern chip are smaller than a virus, and a single chip can carry over 100 billion of them.
Humanity now manufactures tens of trillions of transistors every second, making them the most produced object in history.
Ford's moving assembly line cut the time to build a Model T from over 12 hours to about 90 minutes.
A Boeing 747 contains roughly six million parts, and about half of them are fasteners.
Boeing's Everett factory, built for the 747, encloses more volume than any other building on Earth.
Standardized shipping containers made ocean freight so cheap that moving a t-shirt across the Pacific costs just a few cents.
Lego molds are machined so precisely that bricks made decades apart still click together, with tolerances of a few thousandths of a millimeter.
Walls of an aluminum drink can are thinner than two sheets of paper, yet the can withstands about three times the pressure in a car tire.
The Gimli Glider, a Boeing 767, ran out of fuel mid flight in 1983 after a pounds to kilograms mix up, then glided to a safe landing.
Metal fatigue cracks spreading from openings in the fuselage brought down the world's first jetliner, the de Havilland Comet, and rewrote aviation design.
Allied engineers built a working prototype vessel from pykrete, a mix of ice and sawdust tough enough to stop bullets, for a planned ice aircraft carrier.
American yards launched Liberty ships at a rate of one every few days in World War 2. One was assembled in under five days as a stunt.
For D-Day, the Allies towed two entire prefabricated harbors across the English Channel, breakwaters and floating piers included.
In 1845 the Royal Navy settled the propeller versus paddle wheel debate with a literal tug of war between two ships. The propeller won.
Train wheels have no differential. Their slightly cone shaped treads automatically steer them around curves.
Modern railway track is welded into ribbons kilometers long, stretched during installation so summer heat cannot buckle it.
Tunnel boring machines are traditionally given women's names before they are allowed to start digging.
Elisha Otis sold the world on elevators in 1854 by standing on a hoisted platform and ordering the rope cut. His safety brake caught him.
IBM's first hard drive, shipped in 1956, stored five million characters and weighed about a ton. It moved by forklift.
In 1947, engineers found an actual moth jamming a relay in the Harvard Mark 2 computer and taped it into the logbook as the first real bug.
The first transatlantic telegraph cable of 1858 worked for barely a month before its chief electrician wrecked it with too much voltage.
Around 99 percent of intercontinental internet traffic travels through cables on the ocean floor, some no thicker than a garden hose.
Corrosion quietly costs the world economy an estimated 3 percent of global GDP every year.
Nearly two billion years ago in Gabon, natural uranium deposits went critical and ran as nuclear reactors, no engineers required.
Itaipu Dam on the border of Brazil and Paraguay supplies nearly 90 percent of Paraguay's electricity.
Venice stands on millions of wooden piles driven into mud centuries ago. Starved of oxygen, the wood has hardened instead of rotting.
Roman surveyors gave the aqueduct to Nimes an average drop of just 25 centimeters per kilometer, held across 50 kilometers of terrain.
For about 3,800 years the Great Pyramid of Giza was the tallest structure on Earth, and its base is level to within a couple of centimeters.
Copper skin on the Statue of Liberty is just 2.4 millimeters thick, hung on an internal iron frame engineered by Gustave Eiffel.
Kola Superdeep Borehole reaches over 12 kilometers down, the deepest hole ever drilled, yet its opening is only about 23 centimeters wide.
Scotland's Falkirk Wheel rotates boats between two canal levels using about as much electricity as boiling eight kettles of water.
Blade tips on a large wind turbine sweep through the air at more than 250 kilometers per hour.
A nuclear power plant is, at heart, an elaborate way to boil water. The reactor's only job is making steam to spin a turbine.
ITER, the fusion reactor being assembled in France, will weigh about 23,000 tons, roughly three Eiffel Towers of machine.
Fusion experiments routinely hold plasma at over 100 million degrees Celsius, many times hotter than the core of the sun.
Troll A, a Norwegian gas platform 472 meters tall, is the tallest structure ever moved by humans. Tugboats towed it out to sea in 1996.
Seawise Giant, the longest ship ever built, measured 458 meters. Stood on end, it would overtop the Empire State Building.
